description: Tomato Late Blight, caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans, is a destructive disease that affects leaves, stems, and fruits. It thrives in cool, moist conditions and spreads rapidly through spores. Symptoms include dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems, often with a white, fuzzy mold on the undersides.
organic_treatment: Apply copper-based sprays (e.g., Bordeaux mixture) weekly as a preventive measure.
Use neem oil or potassium bicarbonate sprays to suppress spore germination.
Remove and destroy infected plant debris to prevent spore spread.
Improve air circulation by pruning dense foliage to reduce humidity.
chemical_treatment: Apply systemic fungicides containing chlorothalonil or mancozeb at the first sign of infection.
Use propiconazole or fludioxonil for foliar applications, following label instructions.
Rotate fungicides to prevent resistance development.
prevention: Plant resistant tomato varieties if available in your region.
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base of plants to keep foliage dry.
Space plants adequately to ensure good air circulation.
Remove volunteer tomato plants and crop residues after harvest.
note: Late Blight spreads rapidly in wet conditions. Immediate action is critical to prevent total crop loss. Monitor weather forecasts and apply preventive treatments during periods of high humidity or rain.
